text: Developing high-energy-density cathode materials that maintain structural integrity during the rapid intercalation and deintercalation of sodium ions remains a significant challenge for the advancement of sodium-ion batteries. In this study, we report the design of a P2-layered oxide cathode, Na₀.₇Li₀.₀₃[Mg₀.₁₅Li₀.₀₇Mn₀.₇₅]O₂, featuring a unique lithium dual-site substitution where Li occupies both transition-metal (TM) and alkali-metal (AM) sites. Using a combination of density functional theory calculations and advanced experimental characterization, we demonstrate that Li ions in the TM sites create Na−O−Li electronic configurations that activate and enhance anionic oxygen redox, significantly boosting specific capacity. Simultaneously, Li ions in the AM sites act as rigid LiO₆ prismatic pillars that stabilize the layered framework by preventing deleterious phase transitions typically observed at high and low voltages. Consequently, this material delivers a high specific capacity of 266 mAh g⁻¹ while maintaining a nearly zero-strain characteristic (only 1.2% volume variation) across a wide 1.5–4.6 V potential range. These results illustrate that a dual-site substitution strategy can effectively resolve the traditional trade-off between capacity and structural stability in advanced rechargeable battery cathodes.
